summary:
The Chasséen culture was the dominant Middle Neolithic culture of much of France, dating to approximately 4500–3500 BCE and named after the site of Chassey-le-Camp in Burgundy. It spread across a large portion of France from the Atlantic coast to the Mediterranean, representing a broadly unified material culture tradition despite considerable regional variation. The Chasséen culture is recognized as a distinct phase in the long cultural sequence of Neolithic France, following earlier LBK and related traditions and preceding the late Neolithic cultures that culminated in the Chalcolithic.

Chasseen communities were farmers cultivating wheat and barley and herding cattle, sheep, pigs, and goats. They constructed elongated rectangular enclosures — causewayed camps — that served as gathering places for seasonal communal activities including feasting, trade, and ritual. Long-distance exchange networks distributed exotic materials including Grand Pressigny flint, jadeitite axes from the Alps, and Mediterranean spondylus shells across the Chasseen cultural zone, reflecting the extensive social connections maintained by Neolithic communities across western Europe.

The Chasséen culture overlapped geographically and temporally with the [[event:35|megalithic traditions]] of Atlantic France, and Chasseen communities both built and used megalithic monuments. Their material culture reflects a mature Neolithic tradition with well-developed craft production, complex mortuary practices, and participation in the broader pan-European exchange networks of the fifth millennium BCE.
